scenarios.title: TCP: 3-Way Handshake → Data Transfer → FIN Teardown
scenarios.description: Complete TCP connection lifecycle: 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) with ISN synchronization, HTTP request/response data transfer with sequence number tracking, and graceful 4-way FIN teardown with TIME_WAIT.

scenarios.title: Lossless Ethernet: PFC Pause → ECN Marking → DCQCN Rate Control
scenarios.description: How RoCEv2 achieves lossless transport: PFC PAUSE frames prevent buffer overflow, ECN marks packets with Congestion Experienced instead of dropping, and DCQCN closes the feedback loop with CNP-triggered rate reduction and timer-based recovery.

scenarios.title: NVMe-oF/RDMA: Discovery → Fabrics Connect → NVMe Write & Read over RoCEv2
scenarios.description: Complete NVMe over Fabrics using RDMA transport: CM connection setup, Fabrics Connect to Discovery Controller, Discovery Log retrieval via RDMA WRITE, IO Controller queue setup, NVMe Write (data via RDMA READ) and NVMe Read (data via RDMA WRITE). Zero-copy data path throughout.

scenarios.title: iSCSI: Login → Discovery → SCSI INQUIRY → WRITE(10) → READ(10)
scenarios.description: Complete iSCSI session: TCP connection, two-stage Login negotiation (security + operational), SCSI INQUIRY to discover LUN, WRITE(10) with R2T flow control, and READ(10). The most widely deployed SAN protocol — standard TCP/IP, no special hardware.

scenarios.title: RoCEv2 RC: Link Training → QP Connection → RDMA WRITE → RDMA READ
scenarios.description: Complete RoCEv2 Reliable Connected exchange over 100GbE: physical link, ARP, CM bring-up, RDMA WRITE, RDMA READ.

scenarios.title: FC Fabric: FLOGI → Name Server → PLOGI → PRLI → SCSI I/O
scenarios.description: Complete Fibre Channel SAN login sequence through a fabric switch: physical link, FLOGI, Name Server discovery, PLOGI, PRLI, then SCSI INQUIRY, WRITE(10), and READ(10).

scenarios.title: FC FLOGI Reject: When Fabric Login Fails
scenarios.description: Error scenario: HBA sends FLOGI and the switch responds with LS_RJT instead of ACC. Demonstrates three common failure causes — zone configuration error, port type mismatch (F-port vs E-port), and duplicate WWPN — with reason codes, diagnostic guidance, and CLI fix examples.

scenarios.title: FC Buffer Credits: Lossless Flow Control Without Pause Frames
scenarios.description: FC's unique credit-based flow control: BB_Credit negotiation during FLOGI, R_RDY credit replenishment in steady state, credit starvation under congestion, and recovery. Contrasts FC buffer-to-buffer flow control with Ethernet PFC and TCP window-based approaches.

scenarios.title: FC-NVMe: NVMe over Fibre Channel — Discovery → Connect → I/O
scenarios.description: Complete FC-NVMe flow on 32GFC: FLOGI, PLOGI, PRLI with NVMe type 0x28 (the key difference from FCP/SCSI), Discovery Controller via FC-NVMe LS, Discovery Log Page, I/O Controller connect, Identify Controller/Namespace, NVMe Write (CMD IU → XFER_RDY → DATA IU → RSP IU) and Read (CMD IU → DATA IU → RSP IU). Compares with both traditional FCP and NVMe-oF/RDMA throughout.

scenarios.title: NVMe/TCP: mDNS → CDC Discovery → DIM → Subsystem Connect → I/O
scenarios.description: Complete NVMe over TCP flow with three-tier discovery: mDNS → CDC (DIM registration, DDC referrals) → DDC on storage (IO Controller details) → IOC connection, Identify Controller/Namespace, NVMe Write and Read.

scenarios.title: GPUDirect RDMA: RoCEv2 WRITE Direct to GPU Memory
scenarios.description: GPUDirect RDMA showing RDMA WRITE landing directly in GPU HBM via nvidia-peermem, bypassing host DRAM entirely. Covers cuMemAlloc, GPU BAR1 exposure, MR registration against GPU memory, and the zero-copy DMA data path.

scenarios.title: GPUDirect Storage: NVMe Read Direct to GPU Memory (Local PCIe)
scenarios.description: GPUDirect Storage (GDS) showing NVMe SSD data read directly into GPU memory via cuFile API and nvidia-fs, bypassing the CPU page cache entirely. Contrasts traditional storage path (3 copies) with GDS zero-copy P2P DMA.

scenarios.title: S3/RDMA: Object PUT and GET over RoCEv2 (NVIDIA Library Architecture)
scenarios.description: Pre-standard S3/RDMA flow using NVIDIA RDMA for S3-compatible storage library: Library Init, QP connect via UD SEND, S3 PUT (inverted — client RDMA WRITEs to server), S3 GET (server RDMA WRITEs to client). Zero CPU data path. Contrasts with traditional S3 over TCP/HTTP.

scenarios.title: GPUDirect Storage: Remote NVMe-oF Read/Write to GPU Memory over RoCEv2
scenarios.description: GPUDirect Storage over RoCEv2 fabric: cuFileRead triggers NVMe-oF READ with RDMA WRITE landing data directly in GPU HBM; cuFileWrite triggers NVMe-oF WRITE with RDMA READ pulling data from GPU HBM. Zero host DRAM bounce.

scenarios.title: ARP & NDP: IPv4 Address Resolution and IPv6 Neighbor Discovery
scenarios.description: Side-by-side comparison: ARP broadcast Request/Reply and Gratuitous ARP (IPv4) vs NDP solicited-node multicast Neighbor Solicitation/Advertisement and Router Advertisement with SLAAC (IPv6). Shows why NDP is more scalable and secure than ARP.

scenarios.title: SMB Direct: Negotiate → RDMA Channel → File Read & Write over RoCEv2
scenarios.description: SMB Direct (MS-SMBD) over RoCEv2: SMB Direct negotiate, SMB 3.1.1 dialect, session/tree setup, then file WRITE (server RDMA READs data from client buffer) and READ (server RDMA WRITEs data into client buffer). Zero-copy data path for Hyper-V over SMB workloads.

scenarios.title: TLS 1.3: Full Handshake → Encrypted Application Data → Alert Close
scenarios.description: Complete TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446) flow: TCP handshake, 1-RTT TLS handshake with x25519 key exchange (ClientHello key_share + ServerHello key_share), encrypted server flight (EncryptedExtensions + Certificate + CertificateVerify + Finished), application data over AES-256-GCM, and graceful close_notify teardown.

scenarios.title: GPU-to-GPU RDMA: Tensor Transfer across Nodes via RoCEv2
scenarios.description: End-to-end GPU-to-GPU tensor transfer using GPUDirect RDMA over RoCEv2: CUDA alloc, nvidia-peermem BAR1 exposure, MR registration, QP bring-up, RDMA WRITE (push) and RDMA READ (pull) — all GPU HBM to GPU HBM with zero host DRAM bounce. Contrasts traditional 4-copy path with GPUDirect 1-copy path.

scenarios.title: Checking Your Email: What Really Happens When You Click Inbox
scenarios.description: You click your Gmail bookmark. Before a single email appears, ~20 protocol exchanges happen in under 500ms: DNS lookup, TCP 3-way handshake, TLS 1.3 encryption, and HTTP/2 multiplexed loading of HTML, JavaScript, and inbox data — all over one connection. Seven protocols, one click.

scenarios.title: NCCL AllReduce: Ring Algorithm over RoCEv2 (4 GPUs)
scenarios.description: NCCL Ring-AllReduce across 4 GPU nodes via RoCEv2: Reduce-Scatter phase (3 steps, accumulate partial gradient sums) then AllGather phase (3 steps, broadcast final result). All data movement via GPUDirect RDMA WRITE — GPU HBM to GPU HBM, zero CPU copies. The dominant communication pattern in data-parallel AI training.

scenarios.title: IPv6 SLAAC: Router Advertisement → DAD → Address Autoconfiguration
scenarios.description: How IPv6 hosts configure their own addresses without a DHCP server: Router Solicitation, Router Advertisement with prefix info, EUI-64 and privacy address generation, Duplicate Address Detection via solicited-node multicast, and RDNSS for DNS. Contrasts with IPv4's DHCP dependency throughout.

scenarios.title: IPv6 vs IPv4 Header: Why IPv6 Is Faster on the Wire
scenarios.description: Side-by-side comparison: IPv4's 13-field variable-length header with per-hop checksum recalculation vs IPv6's 8-field fixed 40-byte header with no checksum and no router fragmentation. Covers extension header chaining (Hop-by-Hop, Fragment, Routing) and why Flow Labels matter for encrypted traffic.

scenarios.title: Happy Eyeballs: How Your Browser Chooses IPv4 vs IPv6
scenarios.description: The Happy Eyeballs algorithm (RFC 8305) in action: parallel DNS AAAA+A queries, IPv6-preferred connection racing with a 250ms fallback timer, and transparent IPv4 fallback when IPv6 is broken. Shows why the IPv4-to-IPv6 transition mostly works without users noticing.

scenarios.title: DHCPv6: Stateful vs Stateless — When RA Isn't Enough
scenarios.description: Three DHCPv6 modes side by side: Stateless (O=1, M=0) for DNS/NTP info alongside SLAAC, Stateful (M=1) with full SARR 4-message exchange for managed address assignment, and Prefix Delegation where an ISP delegates a /48 to a home router — eliminating the need for NAT.

scenarios.title: NAT64/DNS64: IPv6-Only Client Talking to an IPv4-Only Server
scenarios.description: The key federal IPv6 transition technology: DNS64 synthesizes AAAA records from A records using the well-known prefix 64:ff9b::/96, NAT64 translates IPv6 packets to IPv4 on the wire, and 464XLAT handles legacy apps that hardcode IPv4 addresses. How T-Mobile and federal agencies run IPv6-only networks.

scenarios.title: IPv6 Path MTU Discovery: No More Router Fragmentation
scenarios.description: Why PMTUD is mandatory in IPv6: routers cannot fragment, so ICMPv6 Packet Too Big messages tell the source to use smaller packets. Shows PMTUD working correctly, source fragmentation via Fragment extension header, and the dreaded PMTUD black hole when firewalls block ICMPv6.

scenarios.title: Hello World Chat: Watch Your Message Traverse the Network Stack
scenarios.description: Send a real chat message and watch it get wrapped in WebSocket, TLS, TCP, IP, and Ethernet headers — layer by layer — then travel as bits on the wire. Your first look inside the network stack, live.

scenarios.title: Endianness: Why Networks and CPUs Disagree on Byte Order
scenarios.description: Little-endian vs big-endian: how your CPU stores 0x0050 one way but the network expects it another. See the real bug that happens when you forget htons(), and learn the four functions that fix it.

scenarios.title: FC-SP-3 Secure HBA: Fabric Authentication → Session Key → Encrypted I/O
scenarios.description: A companion to the FC Fabric scenario. Picks up at PLOGI and adds the FC-SP-3 security layer: DH-CHAP mutual authentication, Security Association establishment with ML-KEM-1024 post-quantum key encapsulation, AES-256-GCM session key derivation, and encrypted FC frames — all negotiated automatically as part of PRLI.

scenarios.title: FC-SP-3 DH-CHAP: When Authentication Fails
scenarios.description: Error scenario: DH-CHAP authentication fails due to mismatched shared secrets between HBA and switch. Traces AUTH_Negotiate through DH-CHAP Challenge/Reply, shows the hash comparison failure, AUTH_Reject with reason codes, and LOGO connection teardown. Includes diagnosis steps and security implications.

scenarios.title: [DRAFT] Parallel vs. Serial: From SCSI Buses to PCIe Lanes
scenarios.description: Why did the industry abandon parallel buses in favor of serial links? This scenario walks through a parallel SCSI command exchange, exposes the physical limits that killed the parallel bus, and shows how PCIe solved each problem with a serial differential-pair architecture.

scenarios.title: FC RSCN: How Fabric Topology Changes Propagate to End Devices
scenarios.description: What happens when a host disappears from the fabric? Traces the complete RSCN flow: Host A goes offline, switch detects failure, removes Name Server entries, sends RSCN to registered ports, Host B acknowledges, re-queries the Name Server, discovers Host A is gone, and triggers multipath failover.

scenarios.title: [DRAFT] NVMe/TCP: Full Vertical Stack Trace — Connect → Write
scenarios.description: Every other ProtoViz scenario follows packets horizontally — initiator to target across the wire. This scenario goes vertical: one TCP connection establishment and one 4 KB NVMe WRITE command, traced from user-space syscall down through every kernel layer, across the wire, and back up the target stack to completion.
